canonical_examples.analysis: Before targeting, a monetary aggregate functioned as an informative proxy within an existing financial system. Once authorities selected it for control, banks and markets adapted around the rule, changing the institutional behavior that had produced the statistical relation. The measure ceased to summarize the same underlying process. Goodhart's Law diagnoses this as control-induced metric decay: the intervention on the proxy altered the proxy-goal relationship.
canonical_examples.problem: Charles Goodhart formulated the law in the context of British monetary policy: monetary aggregates that appeared to have stable relationships with economic targets became unreliable once policymakers tried to control them.

canonical_examples.analysis: The true objective is student learning, but the proxy is test performance. Once rewards and sanctions attach to the score, schools can narrow curriculum, teach to the test, exclude weak test-takers, or manipulate administration. Some score gains may reflect learning, but the incentive pressure expands the proxy gap and corrupts the indicator. The remedy is not no measurement; it is lower-stakes, multi-measure evaluation with audits and attention to unmeasured educational goods.
canonical_examples.problem: Donald Campbell used educational testing as a standard example: when test scores become the high-stakes criterion for schools or teachers, the scores become less valid as indicators of learning.

core_claims: A statistical regularity that is useful for observation can break down when used as a control target because the intervention changes the behavior that generated the regularity.
Proxy optimization creates pressure to exploit the gap between the measure and the true objective, especially when agents are rewarded on the proxy.
Metrics fail through distinct mechanisms: regressional extremal selection, causal intervention on the proxy, adversarial gaming, and distributional change.
The stronger the incentive attached to a measure, the faster unmeasured dimensions and data integrity tend to degrade.
A target can be locally optimized while making the underlying system worse, because the proxy omits quality, context, externalities, or long-term effects.
No single fixed metric remains reliable under sustained optimization; robust governance requires metric portfolios, audits, qualitative judgment, and periodic redesign.
Goodhart effects are not mere corruption: even well-intentioned agents can distort outcomes when the measured objective is narrower than the real one.
description: Goodhart's Law explains how proxy measures degrade when used for control, incentives, or optimization, because agents adapt to the metric and the measured correlation with the real goal breaks down.
failure_modes: Metric nihilism: concluding that all metrics are useless, instead of distinguishing passive indicators, weak incentives, and high-stakes targets.
Under-specifying the true objective: without a clear target of value, any complaint about a metric becomes unfalsifiable.
Ignoring base rates and ordinary noise: not every metric reversal is gaming; some failures come from regression to the mean or random variation.
Adding too many metrics: metric portfolios can create confusion, bureaucracy, and new gaming surfaces if not governed.
Believing secrecy solves the problem: hidden metrics reduce some gaming but can also reduce legitimacy, learning, and error correction.

key_vocabulary.Adversarial Goodhart: Failure caused by agents intentionally exploiting a known metric.
key_vocabulary.Campbell's Law: The related social-science claim that high-stakes quantitative indicators become corrupted and distort the processes they monitor.
key_vocabulary.Causal Goodhart: Failure caused when intervening on the measure does not causally improve the true goal.
key_vocabulary.Extremal Goodhart: Failure caused when optimization pushes the system into regions where the proxy-goal relationship no longer holds.
key_vocabulary.Gaming: Behavior that raises the metric while bypassing or damaging the underlying objective.
key_vocabulary.Metric fixation: Organizational overcommitment to quantified indicators at the expense of judgment, context, and unmeasured value.
key_vocabulary.Proxy gap: The difference between what the metric captures and what the principal actually cares about.
key_vocabulary.Proxy measure: An observable variable used because the true objective is hard to measure directly.
key_vocabulary.Regressional Goodhart: Failure caused by selecting extreme measured values where noise, luck, or regression to the mean makes the proxy less representative.
key_vocabulary.Target: A metric value used for incentives, control, ranking, regulation, or optimization rather than passive monitoring.

name: Goodhart's Law
operative_procedure: Name the true objective in ordinary language before naming the metric: specify what outcome, value, or system property the measure is supposed to stand for.
Map the proxy relation: explain why the measure historically correlated with the objective and what assumptions make that correlation hold.
Identify who can see, influence, or benefit from the metric: list agents, incentives, penalties, rankings, and optimization mechanisms.
Diagnose likely Goodhart channels: check for regressional selection, causal mismatch, adversarial gaming, and extremal extrapolation outside the metric's valid range.
Look for side effects and omitted dimensions: ask what important quality, fairness, safety, learning, resilience, or long-term effect is not measured.
Design anti-Goodhart controls: use multiple imperfect metrics, random audits, qualitative review, holdout measures, delayed outcome checks, and penalties for data manipulation.
Keep the metric provisional: rotate, recalibrate, or retire indicators when behavior changes, and avoid making any single proxy the sole target.
Report recommendations by separating measurement, incentives, and governance: say what to measure, what not to reward mechanically, and how to detect metric decay.
scope_and_limits: Goodhart's Law is strongest for incentive design, public administration, machine-learning objectives, education, policing, healthcare, finance, and organizational KPIs where proxies are optimized by adaptive agents. It does not imply that measurement is bad; it warns that measurement and incentive design must be separated, audited, and revised. It is less useful where a measure directly is the desired outcome or where actors cannot affect the measure.
source_summary: Authored from model knowledge of Goodhart, Campbell, Strathern, and contemporary Goodhart taxonomy literature, without fetched source text.

source_texts.title: Charles A. E. Goodhart, Problems of Monetary Management: The U.K. Experience (1975)
Donald T. Campbell, Assessing the Impact of Planned Social Change (1976)
Marilyn Strathern, 'Improving ratings': audit in the British University system (1997)
David Manheim and Scott Garrabrant, Categorizing Variants of Goodhart's Law (2018)

when_to_use: a metric that once tracked a real goal starts producing worse behavior after rewards, punishments, rankings, or targets are attached to it
people can improve the measured number without improving the underlying outcome the number was meant to represent
a dashboard, KPI, test score, model objective, or regulatory threshold is being optimized by actors who know how it is measured
performance management creates gaming, selection effects, neglect of unmeasured work, or degradation of data quality
you need to design controls where the target is only a proxy for value, safety, learning, health, or public welfare
